title: I/O Design Challenges Grow In AI Data Centers And HPC Clusters
gist: As AI workloads scale from single chips to multi-die chiplets and HPC clusters, physical I/O design has become a first-class architectural bottleneck. Choices around connectors, interconnect protocols, packaging, and redundancy now directly determine AI chip commercial viability. New standards like OCP's Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC) and congestion control mechanisms such as DCQCN and Ultra Ethernet's Link Layer Retry are emerging to address reliability and latency demands at scale.

insights.claim: A designer's choice of I/O connectors and interconnect protocols can be the difference between a massively profitable AI chip and a flop.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: I/O IP vendors (Synopsys, Rambus, Cadence) are critical picks-and-shovels plays in the AI chip buildout; their leverage increases as interconnect complexity grows.
insights.quote: A designer's choice of I/O connectors and interconnect protocols can be the difference between a massively profitable AI chip and a flop.

insights.claim: I/O tradeoffs impact airflow, cooling, rack design, and the power coming into the rack, making I/O selection a system-level concern rather than just a chip-level one.
insights.speaker: Arif Khan
insights.so_what: System integrators and data center operators face cascading cost implications from I/O choices; cooling and power infrastructure capex is directly linked.
insights.quote: What tradeoff you make will have an impact on your airflow, cooling, rack design, the power coming into the rack, and so on.

insights.claim: Agentic AI capabilities are being added to EDA tools, but complex physics-based AI for thermal and system implementation problems is not yet mature enough to significantly accelerate design.
insights.speaker: Arif Khan
insights.so_what: EDA AI tooling is a work-in-progress; companies claiming full AI-driven chip design automation for hard physics problems are overstating current capabilities.
insights.quote: The complex, physics-based AI is not all there yet. Some of these are very hard physics problems to solve in terms of system implementations, including thermal.

insights.claim: Chiplets and 3D multi-die packaging present the hardest I/O challenges due to heterogeneous interfaces, signal integrity constraints, and rapidly scaling bandwidth requirements.
insights.speaker: William Wang
insights.so_what: Signal integrity and protocol IP for multi-die assemblies are high-value engineering bottlenecks; companies owning UCIe and BoW IP are well-positioned.
insights.quote: Chiplets and 3D multi-die packaging present the hardest I/O challenges due to heterogeneous interfaces, signal integrity constraints, and rapidly scaling bandwidth requirements.

insights.claim: UCIe and BoW links running at 32, 48, or 64 giga-transfers per second go into bumps whose impedance shifts with temperature and mechanical strain, causing multi-die programs to quietly lose months on integration.
insights.speaker: Ashish Darbari
insights.so_what: Bump-level signal integrity at high giga-transfer rates is an underappreciated design risk that inflates multi-die project schedules and costs.
insights.quote: UCIe and BoW links running at 32, 48, or 64 giga-transfers per second are going into bumps whose impedance shifts with temperature and mechanical strain.

insights.claim: Heterogeneous integration with compute on N2, I/O on N5, SRAM on N3, and analog on N16 requires managing different PDKs, reliability models, and thermal coefficients simultaneously under one workload.
insights.speaker: Ashish Darbari
insights.so_what: Multi-die designs dramatically increase EDA and verification complexity; EDA vendors offering cross-process-node co-simulation tools have a structural advantage.
insights.quote: Heterogeneous integration makes the bookkeeping painful — a compute die on N2, I/O on N5, SRAM on N3, analog on N16 — different PDKs, different reliability models, different thermal coefficients.

insights.claim: Interconnect in multi-die systems is no longer a single choice but a hierarchy of choices across package, board, and rack, including where protocols terminate, where coherency lives, and what traffic stays on-package.
insights.speaker: Lou Ternullo
insights.so_what: System architects must model interconnect as a layered stack decision; IP licensing and chiplet integration services spanning all three tiers are increasingly valuable.
insights.quote: Interconnect is no longer a single choice. It is a hierarchy of choices across package, board, and rack.

insights.claim: In a 3D link with failed hybrid bonds, redundant links can replace them during assembly or in the field, and software can monitor, test, and repair these links.
insights.speaker: Rob Kruger
insights.so_what: Silicon lifecycle management and in-field link repair are becoming mandatory features for advanced packaging IP, raising the bar for HPC-grade chiplet designs.
insights.quote: Say you're doing a 3D link and there's a failed hybrid bond, which could be a problem during assembly. We have redundant links to replace those in the system.

insights.claim: The HPC ecosystem is trying to make a whole cluster of compute nodes act as a single computer, requiring I/Os capable of passing coherent memory data across chips without practical limits.
insights.speaker: Priyank Shukla
insights.so_what: The architectural shift from single-chip to cluster-as-computer is the defining demand driver for next-generation I/O and interconnect IP.
insights.quote: We are trying to create a large cluster that can act as a single, unified compute facility, which is different from what we are doing now.

insights.claim: Beachfront density—the amount of data that can be transferred per millimeter of die edge—is a hard physical limit constraining how fast chiplets can communicate.
insights.speaker: Priyank Shukla
insights.so_what: Beachfront density is an investment-grade constraint; technologies that increase I/O density per mm of die edge (e.g., EMIB, hybrid bonding) directly unlock performance scaling.
insights.quote: The chip has a limited edge on the die, so you want to utilize as much bandwidth from a limited beachfront.

insights.claim: Nvidia's NVL72 exascale system demonstrated that connectivity is king—NVLink and NV switches allow many GPUs to act as one giant GPU, shifting focus beyond pure compute.
insights.speaker: Saurabh Gayen
insights.so_what: Nvidia's interconnect moat (NVLink, NV switches) is a key competitive advantage; rivals must match interconnect bandwidth and latency, not just FLOPS.
insights.quote: NVL72 was a milestone to show connectivity is king.

insights.claim: Data movement and memory access, not compute, are the primary limitations to AI model performance both within and across chips.
insights.speaker: Saurabh Gayen
insights.so_what: Memory bandwidth and interconnect IP are more critical bottlenecks than raw compute FLOPS for AI scaling; HBM vendors and interconnect IP players are structural beneficiaries.
insights.quote: Data movement and memory access are really the problem within a chip, as well as across chips.

insights.claim: AI training requires deterministic, low-latency communication between all chips in a cluster—non-deterministic networking translates directly to poor user experience for AI chatbots.
insights.speaker: Saurabh Gayen
insights.so_what: Deterministic networking (e.g., Ultra Ethernet, InfiniBand) is a prerequisite for production AI clusters; vendors offering deterministic fabrics at scale have a durable advantage.
insights.quote: Not only do you want a crazy amount of low-latency communication between all your chips, but you want it to be deterministic.

insights.claim: GPU clusters generate bursty collective communication library (CCL) traffic that demands very low latency and near-zero loss to avoid GPU idling from network bottlenecks.
insights.speaker: Razvan Arhip
insights.so_what: Network equipment optimized for CCL traffic patterns (low latency, low loss) is a high-priority procurement item for hyperscalers; Ethernet vs. InfiniBand competition intensifies.
insights.quote: GPUs are expensive, and the clusters are expensive, so you don't want to idle them because of the network.

insights.claim: New congestion control mechanisms including DCQCN, Ultra Ethernet Consortium's Link Layer Retry (LLR), and Credit-Based Flow Control (CBFC) have been adopted because upper-layer protocols like TCP can no longer fix losses at data center scale.
insights.speaker: Razvan Arhip
insights.so_what: Ultra Ethernet Consortium standards are gaining traction as TCP proves insufficient for AI cluster networking; Ethernet switch vendors implementing LLR and DCQCN have a near-term design win opportunity.
insights.quote: You can no longer rely on the upper protocols like TCP in the data centers to fix the loss. You need to minimize the congestion that is generating the loss.

insights.claim: OCP's open-source Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC) protocol sprays RDMA traffic across many paths and actively load-balances, eliminating flow collisions, lowering latency, reducing power, and allowing NIC-T0 link loss without killing the training job.
insights.speaker: Liz Allan
insights.so_what: MRC adoption by a coalition including AMD, Broadcom, Intel, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and OpenAI signals a credible open alternative to proprietary interconnects; network silicon vendors supporting MRC gain access to a broad hyperscaler customer base.

insights.claim: In large-scale AI training, the network is a first-class architectural element, not just plumbing—bandwidth, latency, topology, congestion behavior, and fault tolerance all determine which workloads scale efficiently.
insights.speaker: Ashish Darbari
insights.so_what: Networking infrastructure vendors (switches, NICs, cables) deserve an architectural valuation premium in AI infrastructure spending, not just a commodity multiple.
insights.quote: In a cluster, the network is not just plumbing. It is a first-class architectural element.

themes.theme: Telemetry, Reliability, and Silicon Lifecycle Management

insights.claim: Silicon lifecycle management features—including sensors for process, voltage, temperature, and signal integrity with hardware-first aggregation—have moved from nice-to-have to must-have in HPC.
insights.speaker: Rob Kruger
insights.so_what: Embedded telemetry and lifecycle management IP is now a qualification requirement for HPC/AI chip designs; vendors offering OCP-compliant silicon lifecycle IP have a near-term upsell opportunity.
insights.quote: In HPC, things that were nice to haves before, like silicon life cycle management, now are must-haves.

insights.claim: Aggregating telemetry from thousands of I/Os in a data center creates a coordination challenge, with hardware-first approaches preferred over pure software to avoid performance overhead.
insights.speaker: Rob Kruger
insights.so_what: Hardware-accelerated telemetry aggregation is an emerging IP category; it represents a differentiation opportunity for data center chip vendors beyond raw compute.
insights.quote: If you have thousands of I/Os in the data center, all running software. You might choose a hardware-first approach instead.

themes.theme: EDA and Signal Integrity Learning Curve

insights.claim: The learning curve for new packaging technologies requiring system-level and budget-type analysis is demanding, but mastering it is the core factor for success in gaining market share.
insights.speaker: Hee Soo Lee
insights.so_what: EDA providers with strong signal integrity and packaging analysis tools (Keysight EDA, Cadence, Synopsys) are indispensable to design teams navigating rapid technology transitions.
insights.quote: The learning curve is demanding, but that's the core factor for success — getting into a new market, and getting market share out of these harsh market conditions.

insights.claim: Distance between devices across different integration tiers—from millimeters inside a package to meters within a rack—makes routing and congestion management fundamentally harder as more devices are connected.
insights.speaker: Satish Radhakrishnan
insights.so_what: Multi-scale routing and signal integrity tools that span package-to-rack distances are a growing requirement; EDA vendors addressing this full stack will command premium pricing.
insights.quote: Interconnect protocols span very different physical distances, from millimeters inside a package to meters within a rack.
